chunk_summary: Guidelines for adolescent obesity management recommend pharmacotherapy as an adjunct to lifestyle therapy and referral for metabolic surgery in severe cases with comorbidities after therapy failure.
learning_objectives: Understand criteria for initiating pharmacotherapy in adolescents with obesity.
Learn referral guidelines for pediatric metabolic and bariatric surgery.
Identify comorbidities and risk factors influencing therapy escalation in adolescent obesity.
key_facts: Pharmacotherapy is indicated for adolescents ≥12 years with class 2–3 obesity or obesity with worsening T2DM, MASLD, or HTN risk.
GLP-1 receptor agonists are recommended as adjuncts to intensive lifestyle therapy for eligible adolescents.
Metabolic surgery referral is advised for adolescents >12 years with class 2 obesity plus significant comorbidities or class 3 obesity after 6 months of failed therapy.
Screening for depression, disordered eating, and syndromic obesity is essential before initiating pharmacotherapy.
Referrals should be made to high-volume centers with pediatric anesthesia, nutrition, and mental health support.
reference_quote: Refer for pediatric metabolic and bariatric surgery evaluation when the adolescent is >12 years with class 2 obesity plus significant comorbidities or class 3 obesity after failure of intensive lifestyle therapy and appropriate pharmacotherapy for about 6 months. (American Society for Metabolic and Bariatric Surgery, 2018)
document_key_topics: Role of GLP-1 receptor agonists in adolescent obesity
